stage.name: Trading Fundamentals
stage.description: Order books, RSI/MACD/Bollinger, Kelly Criterion, perpetuals, Sharpe ratio, and trading psychology.

elements.content: Market Structure
How price is formed and how to read it

Order Book Mechanics

Bids: buyers waiting at price levels
Asks: sellers waiting at price levels
Spread: gap between best bid & ask

Market order: execute immediately at current price
Limit order: wait until price reaches your level

Large walls in the order book = support/resistance.
Watch for 'walls' being pulled right before a move.

Reading Candlesticks

Each candle = Open · High · Low · Close

Patterns that matter:
• Doji: indecision — buyers/sellers balanced
• Hammer: reversal signal at support
• Engulfing: strong momentum shift
• Long wick: price rejection at that level

Patterns need volume confirmation to be reliable.

actions.text: Before you place a single trade, you need to understand how prices are actually formed.
Market structure — the order book, candlesticks, and what they reveal about supply and demand.

Order book mechanics: bids are buyers waiting at specific price levels. Asks are sellers. The spread is the gap between best bid and best ask. Volume profile shows where the most trades happened historically — these become support and resistance levels.

Reading candlesticks: each candle shows Open, High, Low, and Close for a time period. The doji pattern — open and close nearly equal — signals indecision. A pin bar with a long wick shows price rejection. Engulfing patterns signal potential reversals.

elements.content: Technical Indicators
What RSI, MACD, and Bollinger Bands actually measure

RSI & MACD

RSI = avg gain / avg loss over 14 periods
• >70: overbought (potential reversal)
• <30: oversold (potential bounce)
• Divergence: price makes new high, RSI doesn't = bearish signal

MACD = 12-day EMA − 26-day EMA
Signal line = 9-day EMA of MACD
Crossover above signal line = bullish

Bollinger Bands

Middle: 20-day moving average
Upper/Lower: ±2 standard deviations

Squeeze = low volatility → breakout incoming
Touch of upper band = overbought
Touch of lower band = oversold

Bands expand during trends, contract during consolidation. Width = volatility indicator.

actions.text: Technical indicators don't predict the future — they describe what has happened. Understanding what they measure helps you use them correctly.
RSI, MACD, and Bollinger Bands — the three most widely used indicators in crypto trading.

RSI measures relative strength over 14 periods. Above 70 is overbought — potential reversal. Below 30 is oversold. MACD is two moving averages — the crossover of the MACD line over the signal line indicates momentum shifts. Divergence between price and MACD is a powerful leading signal.

Bollinger Bands: a 20-day moving average in the middle, plus or minus two standard deviations. A Bollinger Squeeze — bands tightening — means volatility is compressing. What follows is usually a significant move. Bands don't tell you the direction, just that a move is coming.

elements.content: Position Sizing
The single most important skill in trading
Position Size = Risk $ ÷ Stop %

Risk $ = Account × Risk Per Trade (e.g. 2%)
Stop % = Distance from entry to stop-loss

Example: $10K account, 2% risk, 5% stop
→ Risk $ = $200 · Position = $200 ÷ 0.05 = $4,000
Kelly Criterion: f = (bp − q) / b where b=odds, p=win rate, q=loss rate. Use half-Kelly in practice to account for estimation error.

actions.text: Position sizing is the most important skill in trading — not entry timing, not indicator selection. Size determines your survivability.
The formula is simple. The discipline to follow it is not.
Position size equals your risk dollars divided by your stop percentage.

Risk dollars equals your account value times your chosen risk per trade — typically 1 to 2 percent. If you have 10,000 dollars and risk 2 percent, that's 200 dollars. If your stop is 10% from entry, your position is 2,000 dollars.

The Kelly Criterion maximizes long-run growth mathematically. In practice, use half-Kelly — full Kelly is too aggressive for anything with variance. Most professional traders risk between 0.5 and 2% per trade.

elements.content: Trading Expectancy
Profitable trading is about math, not prediction
Expectancy = (W% × Avg Win) − (L% × Avg Loss)

A 40% win rate CAN be profitable with high reward/risk
Example: 40% × $300 − 60% × $100 = +$60 per trade

Most traders blow up not from bad win rates — from letting losers run and cutting winners short.
Sharpe Ratio = (Return − Risk-Free Rate) / Standard Deviation. A Sharpe > 1.0 is considered good. Most retail traders have negative Sharpe.

actions.text: Profitable trading is not about being right most of the time. It's about math — specifically expectancy.
A 40% win rate CAN be highly profitable. A 70% win rate CAN be a losing system. It depends on your reward-to-risk ratio.
Expectancy equals win percentage times average win, minus loss percentage times average loss.

Example: 40% wins at 300 dollars average, 60% losses at 100 dollars average. Expectancy equals 40% times 300 minus 60% times 100 — that's positive 60 dollars per trade. Over 100 trades, that compounds.

The Sharpe ratio measures return per unit of risk. A Sharpe above 1.0 is considered good. The best trading systems aim for 2 or above. If your system has a Sharpe below 0.5, you're taking on too much risk relative to your returns.

elements.content: Perpetual Futures & Leverage
The most dangerous — and most used — crypto instrument

Perpetual Futures

Perpetuals have no expiry — unlike monthly futures.

Funding rate keeps price anchored to spot:
• Positive funding (>0): Longs pay shorts — market is net long
• Negative funding (<0): Shorts pay longs — market is net short

High positive funding = crowd is long = contrarian short signal.

Liquidation Mechanics

10× leverage on $1,000 = $10,000 position
A 10% adverse move = 100% loss (liquidation)

Liquidation price = Entry × (1 − 1/Leverage)

Exchange liquidation cascade: bots liquidate the largest positions first, pushing price further, liquidating more.
$1B+ liquidated in single-day events.

actions.text: Perpetual futures are the most traded crypto instrument — and the most dangerous for beginners.
Understanding funding rates and liquidation mechanics is not optional if you trade perps.

Perpetual futures have no expiry date — unlike monthly futures. The funding rate is a periodic payment between longs and shorts that keeps the perp price anchored to the spot price. When funding is highly positive, longs are paying shorts — a contrarian signal.

Liquidation mechanics: 10x leverage on 1,000 dollars controls a 10,000 dollar position. A 10% adverse move equals 100% loss — full liquidation. At 25x leverage, you're liquidated on a 4% move. The exchange takes your collateral. Overleveraging is how traders blow up accounts.

elements.content: Copy Trading & Psychology
Systematic edges and the hardest part — your own mind

Copy Trading

Follow proven traders automatically. Risk: traders change strategy, over-optimized past performance, slippage on large follower bases.

Trading Psychology

Fear → early exits. Greed → holding losers. Revenge trading → blowups. The edge is discipline, not prediction.

OnlyCrypto Traders

Verified traders on platform. Track record is public. Followers share in profit. Copy-stop on drawdown threshold.

actions.text: Copy trading and psychology — two topics that determine real-world trading outcomes more than any indicator.
Following a proven trader is valid strategy. But the hardest part is always your own psychology.

Copy trading: follow proven traders automatically. The risk is that past performance isn't guaranteed. Traders change strategies. Over-optimized backtests don't hold up live. On OnlyCrypto, trader track records are public and verified — giving you genuine data to evaluate.

Trading psychology: fear causes early exits — cutting winners short. Greed causes holding losers too long — hoping they'll come back. Revenge trading after a loss is how small losses become catastrophic losses. The edge is in consistency, not cleverness.

OnlyCrypto traders: verified track records, public performance, and followers share in profits through the platform's commission structure. This aligns the trader's incentive with yours — they do better when you do better.

elements.content: Building a Trading System
Rules that remove emotion from the equation
1

Journal Every Trade

Entry reason, size, stop, target. Review weekly. Patterns reveal edge — and mistakes.
2

Max 2% Risk Per Trade

Lose 10 trades in a row at 2% = still 82% of capital. At 10% risk = 35% left.
3

Plan Before Entry

Know your stop and target before you enter. No plan = emotion decides.
4

Review Weekly

Calculate expectancy, win rate, avg R. If negative expectancy after 50+ trades — change strategy.

actions.text: A trading system is a set of rules that removes emotion from the equation. Without rules, emotions decide — and emotions lose.
Four rules that every consistently profitable trader follows.

Journal every trade: entry reason, position size, stop level, and target. Review weekly. Patterns in your journal reveal both your edge and your recurring mistakes — the journal is where improvement happens.

Maximum 2% risk per trade. Lose 10 trades in a row at 2% and you still have 82% of your capital. At 10% risk per trade — which many beginners use — 10 consecutive losses leaves you with 35% remaining.
Plan before entry. Know your stop and your target before you place the trade. No plan means your emotions decide the exit. Always pre-define your risk.
Review weekly: calculate your expectancy, win rate, and average R multiple. If you have a negative expectancy after 50 or more trades, something in your system needs to change. Data, not feelings.

elements.content: 📐 Position Sizing & Kelly Criterion
The math behind how much to risk per trade

The Kelly Criterion gives the mathematically optimal bet size: f = (bp - q) / b, where b = odds ratio, p = win probability, q = loss probability.


In practice, traders use fractional Kelly (25–50% of full Kelly) to reduce variance. Most professional traders risk 1–2% of account per trade. At 2% risk per trade, you can lose 20 trades in a row and still have 67% of your capital remaining.

📐 Kelly Formula
f = (bp - q) / b

⅓ Fractional Kelly
25-50% of Kelly = lower variance

📊 2% Rule
Risk 2% per trade = 50 chances

🧮 Math
20 losses in a row = 67% remains

elements.content: 🤖 Algorithmic Trading Concepts
How systematic strategies remove emotion from trading

Algorithmic trading executes trades based on pre-defined rules — no emotion, no hesitation. Strategies include: trend following (buy when 50MA > 200MA), mean reversion (fade moves beyond 2 standard deviations), arbitrage (exploit price differences across exchanges).


Key concepts: backtesting (test strategy on historical data), overfitting (strategy works on past data but fails live), slippage (real execution vs backtest assumptions).

📊 Trend Following
Buy when price is above MA

↩️ Mean Reversion
Fade extremes, expect return to avg

⚡ Arbitrage
Buy cheap, sell expensive simultaneously

⚠️ Overfitting
Works in backtest, fails in live

questions.question: RSI above 70 typically signals what?

options.label: Oversold conditions — likely to rise
Overbought conditions — potential reversal down
Strong trend continuation
Low volatility — consolidation phase

questions.analysis: RSI > 70 = overbought. RSI < 30 = oversold. These are not guaranteed reversal signals but indicate extreme momentum conditions.

questions.question: You have a $10,000 account and risk 2% per trade. Your stop-loss is 5% below your entry. What is your maximum position size?
What does a funding rate of +0.1% on a perpetual futures contract mean?

options.label: Short traders pay long traders 0.1% every 8 hours
Long traders pay short traders 0.1% every 8 hours
The exchange charges 0.1% for holding overnight
The contract expires with 0.1% slippage

questions.analysis: Positive funding = longs pay shorts. It means the market is net long / bullish — longs pay to keep their positions open.

questions.question: Which TWO are components of a trade's expected value (expectancy)?

options.label: Win rate
Average RSI reading
Average win/loss ratio
Number of indicators used

questions.analysis: Expectancy = (Win Rate × Avg Win) − (Loss Rate × Avg Loss). Both win rate and win/loss ratio are essential.

questions.question: A trader says 'I have a 40% win rate so I must be losing money.' Is this necessarily true? Which answer correctly uses the concept of expectancy?

options.label: Not necessarily — if the average win is $300 and average loss is $100, expectancy is +$60 per trade (40%×$300 − 60%×$100), making the strategy profitable despite the low win rate
Yes — any win rate below 50% means you lose more trades than you win, so you must be unprofitable over time
Not necessarily — a 40% win rate is profitable as long as you trade more than 100 times per month to average out the losses
Yes — expectancy only matters when win rate is above 50%; below that, position sizing cannot compensate

questions.analysis: Expectancy = (Win% × Avg Win) − (Loss% × Avg Loss). With 40% wins at $300 avg and 60% losses at $100 avg: (0.4 × $300) − (0.6 × $100) = $120 − $60 = +$60 per trade. A 40% win rate is perfectly profitable with a high enough reward-to-risk ratio. Most retail traders blow up not from low win rates — but from letting losers run and cutting winners short.

questions.question: What is the Kelly Criterion formula?

options.label: f = (win_rate × avg_win) - loss_rate
f = (bp - q) / b
f = sharpe_ratio / volatility
f = expected_value / risk_per_trade

questions.analysis: The Kelly Criterion gives the optimal fraction of capital to bet: f = (bp - q) / b, where b = odds ratio (profit/loss), p = probability of winning, q = probability of losing (1-p). In practice, traders use 25-50% of Kelly to reduce variance.

questions.question: What is backtesting, and what is its main risk?

options.label: Testing a strategy on live markets with small position sizes — main risk is slippage
Testing a strategy on historical data — main risk is overfitting to past conditions
Comparing two strategies against each other — main risk is survivorship bias
Running a strategy on paper only — main risk is emotional deviation

questions.analysis: Backtesting simulates a strategy on historical price data. The main danger is overfitting — tuning parameters so precisely to past data that the strategy has no predictive power on new data. A strategy with perfect backtest results often fails in live trading.

questions.question: What does the Sharpe Ratio measure?

options.label: The total return of a portfolio over one year
Risk-adjusted return — return per unit of volatility taken
The maximum drawdown from peak to trough
The correlation between two trading strategies

questions.analysis: Sharpe Ratio = (Portfolio Return - Risk-Free Rate) / Standard Deviation of Returns. A Sharpe above 1.0 is considered good — you're earning more return per unit of risk taken. A strategy earning 50% but with extreme volatility may have a worse Sharpe than one earning 20% steadily.

actions.text: Time to test your knowledge! Answer each question carefully — you can review the material after the quiz.
Take your time with these questions. The goal is to solidify your understanding, not just pass the quiz.
